title: Nerves Vocabulary
description: Essential terminology for spinal cord, nerves, reflexes, and sensory pathways

questions.q: The spinal cord extends from the medulla to approximately which vertebral level?
questions.explain: The spinal cord ends at the conus medullaris around L1-L2 in adults. Below this, only nerve roots continue as the cauda equina.

optionExplains.text: T12 would be too high. The cord extends below the thoracic region.
L1-L2 — the conus medullaris terminates here in adults. This is why lumbar punctures below L2 are safe — you tap the cauda equina, not the cord itself.
L5 is below where the cord ends. Only the cauda equina reaches there.
S1 is sacral — far below the cord's termination.

questions.q: The collection of nerve roots below the conus medullaris is called the:
questions.explain: The cauda equina — Latin for 'horse's tail' — is the bundle of lumbar and sacral nerve roots streaming down from where the cord ends.

optionExplains.text: Spinal ganglia contain sensory neuron cell bodies. Not the nerve root bundle.
Cauda equina — lumbar, sacral, and coccygeal nerve roots descending through the vertebral canal. They look like a horse's tail.
Dorsal root is just the sensory branch of one spinal nerve. Cauda equina is many roots.
Filum terminale is the thin strand anchoring the cord to the coccyx. Not the nerve roots.

questions.q: Afferent neurons carry impulses:
questions.explain: Afferent means 'arriving' — these sensory neurons carry information from receptors TO the central nervous system.

optionExplains.text: That's efferent — 'exiting' the CNS. Motor neurons going out.
Afferent neurons bring sensory information IN. Touch, pain, temperature, proprioception — all travel afferent pathways to the CNS for processing.
Those are interneurons — connections within the CNS.
Afferent neurons span the entire sensory path, not just the brain.

questions.q: The cervical plexus innervates structures of the:
questions.explain: The cervical plexus (C1-C4) supplies the neck, posterior head, and most importantly gives rise to the phrenic nerve for the diaphragm.

optionExplains.text: Upper limb is brachial plexus territory (C5-T1).
Cervical plexus — C1-C4 form this network. It supplies skin and muscles of the neck and back of head. The phrenic nerve (C3-C5) emerges here to control breathing.
Lower limb is lumbar and sacral plexuses.
Thoracic wall is supplied by intercostal nerves, not cervical plexus.

questions.q: Which type of receptor detects changes in temperature?
questions.explain: Thermoreceptors detect temperature changes — separate receptors for warmth and cold, located in the skin and hypothalamus.

optionExplains.text: Mechanoreceptors detect pressure, vibration, and stretch — mechanical forces.
Thermoreceptors — specialized free nerve endings responding to temperature. Cold receptors fire faster as temperature drops; warm receptors fire faster as it rises.
Nociceptors detect pain — tissue damage or potentially harmful stimuli.
Proprioceptors detect body position and movement — muscle spindles, Golgi tendons.

questions.q: The largest nerve in the body is the:
questions.explain: The sciatic nerve — as thick as your thumb — exits the pelvis and runs down the posterior thigh. It's the leg's main nerve trunk.

optionExplains.text: Femoral nerve supplies anterior thigh. Large, but not the largest.
Sciatic nerve — formed from L4-S3 roots via the sacral plexus. It's the main nerve of the lower limb, splitting into tibial and common fibular nerves behind the knee.
Vagus is the longest cranial nerve but not the largest in diameter.
Tibial nerve is a branch OF the sciatic. Smaller than its parent.

questions.q: The dorsal root ganglion contains cell bodies of:
questions.explain: Dorsal root ganglia house sensory neuron cell bodies. These pseudounipolar neurons send one branch to the periphery and one into the spinal cord.

optionExplains.text: Motor neuron cell bodies are in the ventral horn of the spinal cord, not ganglia.
Sensory neurons — their cell bodies sit in the dorsal root ganglion, outside the CNS. Each sends a peripheral axon to receptors and a central axon into the cord.
Interneurons are entirely within the CNS. No ganglia for them.
Autonomic neuron cell bodies are in different ganglia — sympathetic chain or prevertebral.

questions.q: A reflex arc requires at minimum:
questions.explain: The simplest reflex — like the knee-jerk — uses just two neurons: Sensory (afferent) and motor (efferent). It's monosynaptic.

optionExplains.text: One neuron can't form a reflex arc — you need at least input and output neurons.
Two neurons minimum — the stretch reflex (knee-jerk) is monosynaptic: Sensory neuron synapses directly on motor neuron. No interneuron needed.
Three neurons would include an interneuron — common but not minimum.
Five is excessive for a simple reflex arc.

questions.q: The brachial plexus is formed by ventral rami of spinal nerves:
questions.explain: The brachial plexus — supplying the upper limb — forms from C5-T1 ventral rami. Remember: Roots, trunks, divisions, cords, branches.

optionExplains.text: C1-C4 form the cervical plexus — neck, not arm.
C5-T1 create the brachial plexus. Five roots combine and reorganize to eventually form the musculocutaneous, median, ulnar, radial, and axillary nerves.
T1-T12 are thoracic — they form intercostal nerves, not plexuses.
L1-L4 form the lumbar plexus — supplies lower limb and abdomen.

questions.q: Which receptor type is located in muscles and tendons to detect body position?
questions.explain: Proprioceptors — muscle spindles and Golgi tendon organs — detect muscle length and tension, telling the brain where your body parts are.

optionExplains.text: Mechanoreceptors is a broad category. Proprioceptors are specific for position sense.
Thermoreceptors detect temperature, not position.
Proprioceptors provide position sense — you know where your hand is without looking. Muscle spindles detect stretch; Golgi tendons detect tension.
Photoreceptors detect light — they're in the retina, not muscles.

questions.q: The gray matter of the spinal cord is shaped like a:
questions.explain: In cross-section, spinal cord gray matter forms a butterfly or H shape — dorsal horns receiving sensory input, ventral horns sending motor output.

optionExplains.text: Not circular. The gray matter has projecting horns.
Butterfly or H-shaped gray matter. Dorsal horns point back (sensory), ventral horns point forward (motor). Lateral horns exist in thoracic and lumbar regions for autonomic neurons.
Not square. The shape has distinct projections.
Not triangular. The horns create an H or butterfly appearance.

questions.q: The neuron that transmits impulses from the CNS to effector organs is called:
questions.explain: Efferent neurons carry impulses AWAY from the CNS to muscles and glands. They're motor neurons.

optionExplains.text: Afferent is sensory — bringing information IN to CNS.
Efferent means 'exiting' — these motor neurons carry commands FROM the CNS to muscles (skeletal, smooth, cardiac) and glands.
Interneurons stay within the CNS, connecting neurons to neurons.
Sensory neuron is another term for afferent — incoming, not outgoing.

questions.q: The myelin sheath in the peripheral nervous system is formed by:
questions.explain: Schwann cells wrap peripheral axons in myelin. Each Schwann cell myelinates just one segment of one axon.

optionExplains.text: Oligodendrocytes myelinate CNS axons, not peripheral nerves.
Schwann cells — PNS myelination. One cell, one internodal segment. This is why peripheral nerves can regenerate better — Schwann cells guide regrowth.
Astrocytes support neurons but don't form myelin.
Microglia are immune cells, not myelin producers.

questions.q: Which cranial nerve has the most extensive distribution in the body?
questions.explain: The vagus nerve wanders from the brainstem to the abdomen, innervating heart, lungs, and most digestive organs. Its name means 'wandering.'

optionExplains.text: Trigeminal supplies face and jaw — extensive but limited to the head.
Facial controls facial expression and some sensation — only the head.
Glossopharyngeal reaches the throat but not beyond.
Vagus — cranial nerve X — 'wanders' through thorax and abdomen. It's the main parasympathetic nerve to heart, lungs, and GI tract.

questions.q: The dermatome is an area of skin supplied by:
questions.explain: Each dermatome is a strip of skin innervated by sensory fibers from one spinal nerve. Dermatome maps help locate spinal cord lesions.

optionExplains.text: Cranial nerves supply face — those aren't called dermatomes.
One spinal nerve per dermatome. The pattern wraps around the body: C5 is shoulder, T4 is nipple line, T10 is umbilicus, L1 is inguinal region.
Plexuses mix nerve roots. Dermatomes trace back to individual spinal levels.
Sympathetic trunk is autonomic. Dermatomes are somatic sensory.

questions.q: The gaps in the myelin sheath where action potentials regenerate are called:
questions.explain: Nodes of Ranvier are gaps between myelin segments where ion channels cluster. Action potentials jump from node to node — saltatory conduction.

optionExplains.text: Synapses are junctions between neurons. Nodes are on single axons.
Nodes of Ranvier — unmyelinated gaps packed with voltage-gated sodium channels. Signals jump from node to node, speeding conduction 100-fold.
Motor end plates are where motor neurons meet muscle. Different structure.
Neuromuscular junctions are neuron-to-muscle synapses. Nodes are on axons.

questions.q: The median nerve passes through which structure at the wrist?
questions.explain: The median nerve traverses the carpal tunnel — a narrow passage at the wrist. Compression here causes carpal tunnel syndrome.

optionExplains.text: Cubital tunnel is at the elbow — the ulnar nerve passes through there.
Guyon's canal is for the ulnar nerve at the wrist, not median.
Carpal tunnel — formed by carpal bones and flexor retinaculum. The median nerve runs through with flexor tendons. Swelling compresses it, causing numbness in thumb-to-ring finger.
Tarsal tunnel is at the ankle — tibial nerve territory.

questions.q: Which type of receptor adapts slowly and provides continuous information?
questions.explain: Tonic receptors adapt slowly, continuing to fire as long as the stimulus persists. They monitor ongoing states like posture.

optionExplains.text: Phasic receptors adapt quickly — they signal changes, then go quiet.
Tonic receptors maintain their firing rate. Proprioceptors are tonic — your brain always needs to know where your limbs are, not just when they move.
Nociceptors are pain receptors — some are tonic, but this isn't the best answer.
Thermoreceptors are actually more phasic — they adapt to temperature changes.

questions.q: The radial nerve provides motor innervation primarily to:
questions.explain: The radial nerve is the 'extensor nerve' — it innervates all arm and forearm extensors, plus brachioradialis.

optionExplains.text: Forearm flexors are median and ulnar nerve territory.
Forearm extensors — triceps, brachioradialis, wrist extensors, finger extensors. Radial nerve damage causes wrist drop (can't extend wrist against gravity).
Hand intrinsics are mostly ulnar nerve, with some median (thenar muscles).
Shoulder abductors are axillary nerve (deltoid) and suprascapular nerve.

questions.q: The lumbar plexus gives rise to which major nerve?
questions.explain: The femoral nerve emerges from the lumbar plexus (L2-L4), supplying anterior thigh muscles and carrying sensation from the medial leg.

optionExplains.text: Sciatic nerve comes from the sacral plexus, not lumbar.
Femoral nerve — largest lumbar plexus branch. It supplies quadriceps (knee extension) and carries sensation from medial thigh and leg.
Tibial nerve is a branch of sciatic — sacral plexus origin.
Common fibular is also from sciatic — sacral plexus.

questions.q: The withdrawal reflex is an example of a:
questions.explain: The withdrawal reflex involves multiple synapses — interneurons coordinate flexor activation across multiple joints to pull away from pain.

optionExplains.text: Monosynaptic has only one synapse (sensory to motor). Withdrawal is more complex.
Polysynaptic — multiple interneurons coordinate the response. You don't just flex one joint; you pull the whole limb away while other reflexes maintain balance.
Stretch reflex is the knee-jerk — monosynaptic, not withdrawal.
Crossed extensor is part of withdrawal but not the whole reflex.

questions.q: Meissner's corpuscles are sensitive to:
questions.explain: Meissner's corpuscles detect light touch and texture discrimination. They're concentrated in fingertips — why you can feel fine detail.

optionExplains.text: Deep pressure is Pacinian corpuscles and Ruffini endings.
Light touch — Meissner's corpuscles are superficial, rapidly adapting. They're dense in fingertips and lips, enabling fine texture discrimination.
Vibration is primarily Pacinian corpuscles (deep) and Meissner's for low-frequency.
Temperature is free nerve endings (thermoreceptors).

questions.q: The phrenic nerve originates from which spinal nerve levels?
questions.explain: The phrenic nerve (C3-C5) innervates the diaphragm. Remember: 'C3, 4, 5 keeps the diaphragm alive.'

optionExplains.text: C1-C2 supply neck muscles, not the diaphragm.
C3-C5 — the phrenic nerve. Damage above C3 stops breathing. This nerve descends through the thorax to reach the diaphragm.
T1-T2 contribute to brachial plexus and sympathetics, not phrenic.
L1-L2 is lumbar — way below where phrenic originates.

questions.q: The tract that carries pain and temperature sensations is the:
questions.explain: The spinothalamic tract carries pain, temperature, and crude touch from body to thalamus. It crosses at the spinal cord level.

optionExplains.text: Dorsal columns carry fine touch and proprioception — discriminative sensation.
Spinothalamic tract — lateral division for pain and temperature, anterior for crude touch. It crosses in the spinal cord, so right-side pain travels up the left tract.
Corticospinal tract is motor — descending commands, not ascending sensation.
Spinocerebellar carries proprioception to the cerebellum for coordination.

questions.q: Which structure contains the cell bodies of motor neurons that innervate skeletal muscle?
questions.explain: Ventral horn gray matter contains somatic motor neuron cell bodies. Their axons exit via ventral roots to reach skeletal muscles.

optionExplains.text: Dorsal horn receives sensory input — it doesn't contain motor neurons.
Ventral horn — motor neurons here send axons out the ventral root, through spinal nerves, to skeletal muscles. Lower motor neuron damage causes flaccid paralysis.
Lateral horn contains autonomic (sympathetic) neurons, not somatic motor.
Dorsal root ganglion contains sensory neuron cell bodies, not motor.
